Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If there’s one thing I’ve learned after years of interviewing software engineers and mentoring people through technical interview preparation, it’s this: there isn’t a shortage of coding interview courses. There’s a shortage of good learning paths.
Search for “best coding interview course,” and you’ll find dozens of articles recommending the same platforms. Most compare pricing, course length, or the number of coding problems included. Very few answer the questions that actually matter.
Which course teaches you to think instead of memorize?
Which one prepares you for real interviews instead of platform-specific tricks?
And which one is actually worth paying for?
To answer those questions, I spent several weeks comparing ten of the most popular coding interview courses available today. I worked through lessons, solved problems, evaluated teaching styles, and looked beyond marketing pages to see which platforms genuinely helped me improve. While no single course is perfect for everyone, a handful consistently stood above the rest.
If you’re preparing for software engineering interviews in 2026, these are the seven resources I’d recommend.
How I Compared These Courses
I didn’t rank these platforms based on popularity or community hype. Instead, I evaluated each one using the same questions I’d ask before recommending it to someone on my own team.
First, does it teach problem-solving or simply provide solutions? Second, does it gradually build difficulty instead of overwhelming beginners? Third, does it prepare you for actual interviews rather than coding challenges in isolation? Finally, does it remain useful once you’ve landed the job?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Those criteria shaped every recommendation below.
1. Educative — Best Overall for Interview Preparation
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Educative remains my top recommendation because it approaches interview preparation as a complete learning journey rather than a collection of coding problems. Instead of immediately throwing hundreds of questions at you, the platform gradually introduces common patterns, explains why they work, and reinforces them through interactive exercises.
One thing I particularly like is how naturally the coding interview material connects with related topics such as data structures, algorithms, object-oriented programming, backend development, and even System Design. That’s valuable because modern software engineering interviews increasingly combine multiple skills rather than testing coding in isolation.
If I were preparing from scratch today, I’d begin with the coding interview course before expanding into System Design and behavioral interview preparation. That combination reflects what many companies now expect from mid-level software engineers.
2. LeetCode — Best for Practice
Press enter or click to view image in full size

LeetCode hardly needs an introduction. It’s still the platform where many engineers spend most of their interview preparation time, and for good reason. The sheer number of problems means you’ll rarely run out of material to practice.
What I’ve learned over the years, however, is that LeetCode works best once you already understand the underlying patterns. Without that foundation, it’s easy to spend hours solving random questions without recognizing why certain techniques keep appearing. That’s one reason I don’t usually recommend beginners start here.
Once you’ve built those fundamentals elsewhere, LeetCode becomes one of the best places to strengthen speed, consistency, and confidence before interviews.
3. Fenzo.ai — Best AI Coding Interview Tutor
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Fenzo.ai approaches interview preparation very differently from traditional platforms. Rather than following a fixed curriculum, it behaves more like a personal tutor. You can ask why a solution works, request easier or harder variations, generate new interview questions, or have your own solutions reviewed in real time.
What impressed me most was the ability to keep asking follow-up questions until everything made sense. That’s something prerecorded courses simply can’t offer. If you’ve ever watched an instructor solve a problem and thought, “I still don’t understand why they chose that approach,” you’ll immediately appreciate the value of an interactive tutor.
I wouldn’t replace a structured course with AI, but I think pairing Fenzo.ai with one creates a much stronger learning experience.
4. NeetCode — Best Structured Problem Roadmap
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One of the reasons NeetCode has become so popular is that it removes much of the guesswork from interview preparation. Instead of wondering which problem to solve next, you’re given a carefully organized roadmap covering the patterns that appear most frequently in coding interviews.
The explanations are concise, the pacing is approachable, and the progression feels deliberate rather than random. That makes it particularly valuable for developers who’ve previously bounced between unrelated LeetCode problems without seeing much improvement.
I still think the platform works best after you’ve learned the fundamentals elsewhere, but it’s an excellent companion for systematic practice.
5. AlgoMonster — Best for Learning Patterns
If there’s one platform that consistently emphasizes pattern recognition, it’s AlgoMonster. Rather than treating every coding problem as unique, it teaches you to recognize recurring techniques like sliding windows, two pointers, binary search, graph traversal, and dynamic programming.
That shift in thinking is incredibly important because interview preparation isn’t about memorizing hundreds of solutions. It’s about recognizing familiar structures inside unfamiliar questions.
For engineers who already understand basic programming but struggle to identify patterns during interviews, AlgoMonster is a strong choice.
6. Interview Kickstart — Best for Intensive Preparation
Interview Kickstart is much more comprehensive than most self-paced platforms. Alongside coding interviews, it includes mock interviews, coaching, System Design, behavioral preparation, and career guidance.
The biggest advantage is accountability. If you learn best through structured schedules and instructor feedback rather than independent study, that additional support can make a real difference.
The obvious downside is cost. Compared with most resources on this list, Interview Kickstart represents a much larger investment, so I’d mainly recommend it for engineers targeting highly competitive roles at major technology companies.
7. Coursera — Best for Algorithm Fundamentals
Coursera offers several university-backed algorithm and data structure specializations that provide an excellent theoretical foundation for coding interviews. While they aren’t interview platforms in the traditional sense, they do an excellent job explaining why algorithms work instead of simply presenting solutions.
That’s particularly valuable if you’ve learned programming through projects but never formally studied computer science. Building stronger algorithm intuition often makes coding interview preparation significantly easier later on.
I see Coursera as a complement rather than a replacement for dedicated interview practice platforms.
Which Coding Interview Course Should You Choose?
The answer depends much more on your background than on the platform itself.
If you’re starting from scratch, I’d recommend Educative because it provides the most complete and structured learning path. If you already understand the basics and simply need more practice, LeetCode remains difficult to beat. If you enjoy conversational learning, Fenzo.ai is one of the most useful AI tutors I’ve tried. For developers who want a clear roadmap, NeetCode is excellent, while AlgoMonster is particularly strong for mastering recurring interview patterns.
The remaining platforms fill more specialized roles. Interview Kickstart is ideal for learners who benefit from coaching and accountability, while Coursera is a great way to strengthen your algorithm fundamentals before diving into interview-specific practice.
Skills Checklist
By the time you’re ready for coding interviews, you should feel comfortable with most of these skills:

• Arrays, strings, linked lists, stacks, and queues

• Trees, graphs, heaps, and hash maps

• Recursion and backtracking

• Binary search

• Sliding window and two-pointer techniques

• Dynamic programming fundamentals

• Graph traversal algorithms

• Time and space complexity analysis

• Communicating your thought process clearly

• Writing clean, readable code under time pressure
If several of these still feel unfamiliar, I’d spend more time strengthening your fundamentals before attempting dozens of mock interviews.
My Biggest Takeaway
One thing this comparison reinforced is that interview success rarely comes from solving the largest number of problems. The engineers I’ve seen perform best aren’t necessarily the ones with the longest LeetCode streaks. They’re usually the ones who understand why solutions work, recognize patterns quickly, and communicate their thinking clearly throughout the interview.
That’s why I increasingly value structured learning over endless problem grinding. Once you’ve developed the right mental models, every new coding question becomes significantly less intimidating.
Final Thoughts
Preparing for coding interviews can feel overwhelming because there are so many resources competing for your attention. It’s tempting to keep switching platforms in search of the perfect course, but that’s rarely what leads to better results.
If I were starting over today, I’d choose one structured learning platform like Educative, use Fenzo.ai whenever I wanted deeper explanations or additional practice, reinforce my skills with NeetCode and LeetCode, and spend the majority of my time writing code instead of comparing courses.
The platform you choose certainly matters, but consistency matters much more. Solve problems regularly, review your mistakes, practice explaining your solutions aloud, and remember that interview preparation is ultimately about becoming a better problem solver — not just passing the next interview.
